title: Arvind Travels
narrative:
Arvind loves to travel. There are n cities in the country where he lives, all located on one straight line numbered 1 to n. The distance between adjacent cities is 1 kilometre, and roads are one-way — you can only travel from city x to city y if x < y.

Arvind is going on a grand tour in his beloved Nissan Micra. The tank capacity is v litres, and the car burns exactly 1 litre per kilometre. The tank starts empty at city 1. In city i, fuel costs exactly i dollars per litre.

Arvind wants to reach city n as cheaply as possible. He can stop and buy fuel at any city along the way. How much will the trip cost?
input_spec:
x is a 2-element list:
  x[0]: n — integer, number of cities (2 ≤ n ≤ 100)
  x[1]: v — integer, tank capacity in litres (1 ≤ v ≤ 100)

City i has fuel price i dollars per litre. The tank starts empty at city 1.
output_spec: Return the minimum total cost (in dollars) to travel from city 1 to city n.

examples.note: Buy 2 litres at city 1 ($2), drive to city 2 (1 litre left), buy 1 litre at city 2 ($2), drive to city 4. Total = $4.
Tank capacity 6 covers the full 6 km. Fill up at city 1 for $6 and drive straight to city 7.
Buy 3 at city 1, top up by 1 at cities 2–7 ($2+3+4+5+6+7), then coast to city 10.
Tank capacity 89 covers 11 km easily. Buy 11 litres at city 1 for $11.
Tank holds only 1 litre — must buy 1 litre at every city 1 through 76. Sum = 1+2+...+76 = 2926.

hints: Fuel prices strictly increase city by city (city 1 is cheapest, city n is most expensive). What does that tell you about WHEN to buy fuel?
Since every future city is more expensive than where you are now, you should always buy as much fuel as you can carry — unless you already have enough to reach city n.
Try simulating: loop through cities 1 to n-1. If current fuel < distance remaining, fill the tank to v. Then subtract 1 for the drive to the next city.
There are only two cases: if v ≥ n-1, just fill n-1 litres at city 1. Otherwise you top up at each city until you have enough to coast the last v cities.
The total cost has a closed form: the sum 1 + 2 + ... + (n-v) plus (v-1) extra from topping up. Simplify to v + sum(2..n-v) = v + (n-v)*(n-v+1)%2 - 1.
winning_criteria: FASTEST correct single-parameter func wins. If within 10% of the fastest timing, shortest code wins. Measured with: `\t:1000 func each x` and `-2+count string func`.
